hints.a verb meaning 'go': 'Go' would be 'vade' (imperative of 'vadere') — 'vae' is an interjection meaning 'woe'
hints.an adverb meaning 'truly': 'Truly' would be 'vere' — 'vae' is an interjection of distress
hints.a conjunction meaning 'or': 'Or' would be 'vel' or 'aut' — 'vae' is an interjection meaning 'woe'

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'victi' — 'victis' with -is is dative/ablative plural
hints.ablative: While 'victis' could be ablative, 'vae' takes a dative of the person affected
hints.genitive: Genitive plural would be 'victorum' — 'victis' is dative with 'vae'

hints.present active participle: Present active participle would be 'vincentibus' — 'victis' with -ct- is perfect passive
hints.gerundive: Gerundive would be 'vincendis' — 'victis' is perfect passive participle used as noun
hints.future active participle: Future active participle would be 'victuris' — 'victis' is perfect passive

hints.to live: That would be 'vivere' — 'victis' comes from 'vincere' (to conquer)
hints.to bind: That would be 'vincire' — similar spelling but 'vincere' means 'to conquer'
hints.to see: That would be 'videre' — 'victis' comes from 'vincere' (to conquer)

hints.vincio, vincire, vinxi, vinctum: These are the parts of 'vincire' (to bind/fetter)
hints.vivo, vivere, vixi, victum: These are the parts of 'vivere' (to live) — similar perfect participle but different verb
hints.video, videre, vidi, visum: These are the parts of 'videre' (to see)

hints.Hannibal of Carthage: Hannibal was famous for crossing the Alps — Brennus said this when weighing gold ransom
hints.Romulus, founder of Rome: Romulus was the legendary founder — Brennus uttered these words after sacking Rome in 390 BC
hints.Cicero, the orator: Cicero lived centuries later — Brennus said this to the defeated Romans
